metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the diagnostic sanitization allowlist baseline in the plan (e.g., which LLM provider response keys are safe to retain, which regex patterns detect hex/base64/email tokens, and what the redaction placeholder string is) so the ADR authoring AC is unambiguously completable.
findings.finding: The diagnostic-sanitization allowlist and redaction rules are not defined anywhere in the plan — the ADR is supposed to author them, but the plan provides no starting content (which top-level keys are safe to include, how hex/base64/email patterns are detected, what the exact redaction token is). This is a real design decision deferred entirely to the executor.
findings.location: Section 'The diagnostic-sanitization ADR' in Acceptance Criteria; also 'Session-log diagnostic write' section.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Sanitization baseline' sub-section specifying: (a) safe top-level keys (e.g. `resolution_class`, `retryable`, `message_truncated_to_N_chars`); (b) strip-all-others default; (c) secondary scan: redact any value matching `[0-9a-fA-F]{32,}` (hex token), base64 pattern, or RFC-5321 email with `<redacted>`; (d) redaction placeholder literal `"<redacted>"`. This gives the ADR author a starting decision to record, not a blank page.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the explicit default for `relates_to` when no ticket_id is available (e.g., 'pass `relates_to=None`; `append_session_log` must accept `None` and omit the field from the log entry').
findings.finding: The session-log calling convention specifies `relates_to=<ticket_id>` with the qualifier 'when available', but gives no default or fallback for the shape-B `_verify_completion()` path where no ticket_id may be in scope — leaving the executor to choose whether to omit the argument, pass None, pass an empty string, or skip the call.
findings.location: Section 'Session-log diagnostic write (calling convention, both shapes)'; AC: 'written to the session log at BOTH the shape-A and shape-B degrade sites'.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'When no ticket_id is in scope (e.g., standalone `rebar verify-completion`), pass `relates_to=None`; `append_session_log` already accepts `None` and omits the field.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For each 'regression test' AC that lacks a named test file, add the target test file path (e.g., `tests/unit/test_gate_dispatch.py` for never-sign guard, `tests/unit/test_mcp_llm.py` for MCP structured failure) so the executor has a concrete proving artifact location.
findings.finding: Multiple ACs specify 'regression test' or 'test for X' without naming the target test file or module, leaving the executor to decide where new tests live — which is a meaningful decision for this plan's integration-owner role.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'never-sign guard (regression test)', 'session-log failure does not fail the gate (regression test)', 'per-criterion fail-open preserved (regression test)', 'test for the mismatch path', 'test for a degraded sub-path' (MCP).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale in the plan for why a live-LLM end-to-end test for the exit-11 path is absent (e.g., 'live-provider 429 testing is covered by mamba's classify_llm_failure test suite; the contract at this layer is the `.retryable` bool, which the unit tests exercise directly').
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new exit-11 code path triggered by live LLM failures (429, connection errors) but provides no rationale for why a live-LLM end-to-end AC is absent, and does not name a child ticket or deferred deliverable that would exercise the retryable path against an actual provider.
findings.location: Section 'Exit-code-11 contract'; AC: '`_review_plan()` exits 11 when the verdict's `coverage.retryable` is true'; AC: `docs/exit-codes.md` entry and `test_exit_codes.py` pin.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite the diagnostic-sanitization ADR AC as an observable outcome (e.g., 'The diagnostic-sanitization ADR is committed and specifies: (a) the explicit key allowlist, (b) the regex patterns for hex/base64/email redaction, (c) the redaction placeholder, and (d) a test that asserts a sample raw exception dict is reduced to only allowed keys with sensitive values replaced') so the criterion is verifiably completable.
findings.finding: The diagnostic-sanitization ADR authoring AC ('is authored') is effort-framed and not verifiably completable in-session because the allowlist/redaction rules it must record are not defined in the plan — there is no observable outcome to check against (any ADR text satisfies 'is authored' regardless of correctness).
findings.location: AC: 'The diagnostic-sanitization ADR (sections: Context, Allowlist + redaction rules, Threat model — keys/PII/hex/base64/email must never leak, Test coverage) is authored.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the exit-code-11 contract's 'Null disposition' line to read '→ the existing PASS / BLOCK / INDETERMINATE exit (i.e., the normal verdict-driven exit code)' so it cannot be misread as excluding exit 2.
findings.finding: The exit-code-11 contract section states 'Null disposition → the existing PASS/BLOCK exit' (mapping to exit 0 or 1), but AC2 and the Shape A description establish that a null disposition with an INDETERMINATE verdict yields exit 2. 'PASS/BLOCK' does not include exit 2, so an implementer reading only the contract table could implement null→{0,1} only and never emit exit 2 for the finders-failed path, directly contradicting AC2 ('exits 2 when INDETERMINATE and coverage.retryable is false/absent').
findings.location: What > Exit-code-11 contract (third bullet) vs. AC2 and What > Two data-flow shapes > Shape A (finders-failed tail)
findings.suggested_fix: Change the third bullet of the exit-code-11 contract from 'Null disposition → the existing PASS/BLOCK exit' to 'Null disposition → the existing verdict-driven exit (0 = PASS, 1 = BLOCK, 2 = INDETERMINATE) — unchanged from today.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend an existing one) that verifies the CLI exit-11 logic is driven by the persisted `coverage.retryable` bool, not by string-matching `resolution_class` — e.g. a test fixture that sets `retryable=True` with a non-WAIT_AND_RETRY class and asserts exit 11 is still produced.
findings.finding: The plan describes a non-trivial implementation constraint — 'the CLIs read the persisted retryable bool directly and never re-derive retryability by string-comparing the class name' — but no acceptance criterion verifies this constraint. An implementation that re-derives retryability from the string class name would pass all ACs but violate this design invariant.
findings.location: What > The single source of retryability: LLMOutcome (second paragraph) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'When coverage.retryable is True and resolution_class is any value (including a hypothetical future class not in {WAIT_AND_RETRY, RETRY_NOW}), _review_plan()/_review_code()/_verify_completion() still exit 11 — verified by a parameterized test that sets retryable=True independently of class name.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a section to the exit-11 ADR (or a dedicated MCP-contract note in docs/ or CLAUDE.md) that specifies the structured-failure return dict schema `{error, resolution_class, retryable, diagnostic}` as the canonical MCP tool error surface and lists it as a versioned contract for driving agents.
findings.finding: The MCP tool structured-failure return — a new API contract for driving agents ({error, resolution_class, retryable, diagnostic}) — is introduced in scope but neither of the two commissioned ADRs (exit-11, diagnostic-sanitization) covers it, and no MCP-specific doc or CLAUDE.md update is specified. An agent relying on `retryable` to branch needs a stable, versioned contract.
findings.location: MCP structured failure return section; AC: '_mcp_llm.py's review_plan/review_code/verify_completion return a structured failure dict'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the exit-11 ADR's Consumers section with a 'MCP tool surface' subsection: field names, types, and the invariant that `resolution_class` is always a member of the 8-value enum or null. Reference it from the `_mcp_llm.py` module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the pre-sign fingerprint recheck invariant — the conditions under which `rebar close` exits 0 unsigned (SHA mismatch between verify and sign) — in either the exit-11 ADR's 'Decision' section or a dedicated entry in docs/exit-codes.md / docs/signing.md, and reference it from the AC for the mismatch path.
findings.finding: The pre-sign fingerprint recheck behavior — closes-unsigned-exits-0 with a stderr warning on SHA mismatch — is a new operator-visible behavioral invariant, but no doc update (not exit-codes.md, not an ADR, not CHANGELOG/CLAUDE.md) is listed to record it. Operators relying on a signed close as a safety signal need to know this silent unsigned path exists.
findings.location: 'Never-sign guard + pre-sign fingerprint recheck' section; AC: 'a mismatch closes unsigned and the close command exits 0 with a stderr warning'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Unsigned-close reasons' table to docs/exit-codes.md (or the exit-11 ADR) listing: (1) --force-close, (2) local-source, (3) code-drifted-between-verify-and-sign. Each row: condition, exit code, stderr text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `_RESOLUTION_MESSAGES` is keyed by the `ResolutionClass` enum member (not its `.value` string), and that a missing-key lookup raises `KeyError` or falls back to a visible sentinel (e.g. `'Unrecognized resolution class — inspect the diagnostic'`), with a test asserting all 8 enum members have entries.
findings.finding: The `_RESOLUTION_MESSAGES` lookup dict is keyed by string class name (e.g. `'WAIT_AND_RETRY'`), not by the `ResolutionClass` enum member. If the enum value is renamed in mamba, the lookup silently returns `None` with no warning, causing a missing or garbled stderr message with no test failure.
findings.location: 'Per-class actionable messages' section; 'Exit-code-11 contract — Message delivery' subsection
findings.suggested_fix: Key the dict by `ResolutionClass` enum instances: `_RESOLUTION_MESSAGES = {ResolutionClass.WAIT_AND_RETRY: '...', ...}` and look up via `_RESOLUTION_MESSAGES.get(outcome.resolution_class, 'Unrecognized resolution class')`. Add a test: `assert set(_RESOLUTION_MESSAGES) == set(ResolutionClass)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling for `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` inside the write lock: on any exception, fall through to the 'closed unsigned / exit 0' path (same as a mismatch) and log a stderr warning, so an I/O error never aborts the close inside the lock.
findings.finding: The pre-sign `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` call inside the write lock has no specified error handling. If the git I/O call raises (e.g., detached HEAD, missing .git, filesystem error), the exception propagates from inside the lock in an unspecified way — potentially aborting the close with a non-zero exit and leaving the transition in an ambiguous state, rather than falling through to the safe 'closed unsigned' outcome the plan defines for a mismatch.
findings.location: What / Never-sign guard + pre-sign fingerprint recheck / close_ticket
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the head_sha call: `try: current_sha = signing.head_sha(repo_root) except Exception: current_sha = None`. Then treat `current_sha is None` (or `current_sha != manifest_sha`) uniformly as 'skip signing, warn, exit 0'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Wrap `classify_llm_failure` in `preflight` with a try/except so that if classification itself raises, `exc.outcome` is left unset (None) and the original `LLMUnavailableError` is still raised cleanly, preserving the getattr-None fallback path.
findings.finding: `classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext(...))` is called in `PydanticAIRunner.preflight` with no fallback if `classify_llm_failure` itself raises. Because the `LLMUnavailableError` `exc` is mutated in-place (`exc.outcome = ...`) before being re-raised, a failure inside `classify_llm_failure` would surface as a different, unclassified exception — not as the expected `LLMUnavailableError` — causing the shape-A degrade write-site to receive a type it doesn't handle and potentially propagate an internal error rather than a structured degraded verdict.
findings.location: What / Two data-flow shapes / Shape A / 'this story ALSO attaches .outcome in preflight'
findings.suggested_fix:
```python
try:
    exc.outcome = classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext(...))
except Exception:
    pass  # outcome stays absent; write-site falls back to plain INDETERMINATE
raise exc
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (or add an AC) that `transition.py`'s error-handling path prints the `CommandError` message string to stderr before returning `exc.returncode`, so the class-specific diagnostic reaches the user on exit 11 from `rebar close` / `rebar transition`.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the class-specific message is 'embedded in the CommandError message' for shape-B (rebar close / rebar transition path), but it does not confirm that transition.py's error handler actually prints that message to stderr before returning exc.returncode. If transition.py only surfaces the returncode and suppresses the message string, users hitting exit 11 from `rebar close` receive no actionable guidance — violating the non-happy-path UX requirement that the class-specific message reaches stderr.
findings.location: Exit-code-11 contract section / Shape B description / AC: 'A degraded verdict/close prints the class-specific message (from the table) to stderr'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: '`rebar close` / `rebar transition` prints the CommandError message to stderr (not just the returncode) when exit 11 fires; verify with a CLI-level test that captures stderr.' If `transition.py` currently swallows the message, add an explicit `click.echo(exc.message, err=True)` (or equivalent) before returning.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit test case to the never-sign guard regression test where `verdict['verdict'] == 'PASS'` but `coverage['resolution_class']` is non-null, and assert `sign_manifest` is NOT called (exercises the new OR branch independently).
findings.finding: The never-sign guard introduces a NEW condition — refuse to sign when `coverage.resolution_class` is set — but the AC's 'regression test' framing only says 'regression test' without specifying a test case where a technically-PASS verdict that carries `resolution_class` is blocked from signing. A test using only a non-PASS verdict would satisfy the existing invariant ('INDETERMINATE is already unsigned') without exercising the new branch. If the new `OR resolution_class is set` condition is silently dropped or miswired, the guard looks green (the non-PASS case still blocks) while the degraded-but-PASS-looking path signs anyway.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'A degraded / INDETERMINATE verdict is emitted unsigned (never-sign guard at the caller refuses to call sign_manifest; regression test)' and 'Never-sign guard' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the never-sign guard regression test, add a fixture that produces `{'verdict': 'PASS', 'coverage': {'resolution_class': 'WAIT_AND_RETRY', 'retryable': True}}` and assert that the sign path is skipped and the output is unsigned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a dedicated test that simulates a preflight `LLMUnavailableError` with a retryable `.outcome` (mocking `classify_llm_failure`) and asserts `_review_plan()` exits 11, covering the preflight-specific path through the shape-A write-site independently of the mid-run path.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new behavior on the `PydanticAIRunner.preflight` path — attaching `.outcome` via `classify_llm_failure` so preflight outages carry disposition and trigger exit 11 rather than plain INDETERMINATE — but no test is cited that exercises this specific path end-to-end (preflight raises → write-site reads `.outcome` → `coverage.retryable=True` → `_review_plan` exits 11). AC #1 ('_review_plan() exits 11 when coverage.retryable is true') can be satisfied entirely by a mid-run mock, leaving the preflight-specific attachment untested. A bug in the `exc.outcome = classify_llm_failure(...)` attachment (e.g., `classify_llm_failure` raises, attribute is misspelled, or the exception type bypasses the attachment) would cause silent fallback to plain INDETERMINATE exit 2 on preflight outages.
findings.location: AC #6 — 'PydanticAIRunner.preflight attaches .outcome (via classify_llm_failure) to its raised LLMUnavailableError' and Shape-A 'Reachability' subsection
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_exit_codes.py` (or a focused unit test for `_degraded_plan_review_verdict`), add a case: mock `PydanticAIRunner.preflight` to raise `LLMUnavailableError` with `.outcome.retryable=True` attached, invoke `_review_plan()`, assert exit code 11. Add a second case with `.outcome=None` (simulating pre-fix preflight) and assert exit code 2 to document the fallback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the preflight `.outcome`-attachment mechanism: replace the description 'raise LLMUnavailableError(...) becomes exc.outcome = ...; raise exc' with the actual fix — wrap `_import_pydantic_ai()` and `_pai_check_config()` in a try/except in `preflight()` to catch the `LLMConfigError` they raise, attach `.outcome`, and re-raise.
findings.finding: The plan's preflight `.outcome`-attachment fix misstates the code path. It describes the change as '`raise LLMUnavailableError(...)` becomes `exc.outcome = classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext(...)); raise exc`', implying `preflight()` contains a `raise LLMUnavailableError(...)` statement. In reality, `PydanticAIRunner.preflight()` (runner.py:163-167) contains only `_import_pydantic_ai()` and `_pai_check_config(self._config)`, which raise `LLMConfigError` (a subclass) deep inside helper functions — not an inline `raise LLMUnavailableError`. The actual fix must wrap the helper calls (e.g. `try: _import_pydantic_ai(); _pai_check_config(...) except LLMUnavailableError as exc: exc.outcome = ...; raise`) — a different mechanical approach than stated. An implementor following the plan literally will search for `raise LLMUnavailableError` in `preflight()`, find nothing, and be unable to apply the change.
findings.location: Plan section 'Two data-flow shapes / Shape A / Reachability (verified)'; AC line 'PydanticAIRunner.preflight attaches .outcome'
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's Shape A section, replace: '`raise LLMUnavailableError(...)` becomes `exc.outcome = classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext(...)); raise exc`' with: 'wrap the two helper calls inside `preflight()` in `try: _import_pydantic_ai(); _pai_check_config(self._config) / except LLMUnavailableError as exc: exc.outcome = classify_llm_failure(exc, ClassifyContext()); raise`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `shim._verdict_to_review_result` must be updated FIRST (AC#4) before `_review_code()`'s exit-11 mapping (AC#3) can work — the top-level `result['coverage']` key does not currently exist in the shim output, so both changes are sequentially dependent.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `_verdict_to_review_result` in `shim.py` produces a `review_result` whose top-level `coverage` can be read by `_review_code()` for exit-11 mapping, and that the fix is to 'COPY the gate verdict's `coverage` (with `resolution_class`/`retryable`/`diagnostic`) to the TOP-LEVEL `review_result['coverage']`'. However, the current `_verdict_to_review_result` (shim.py:78-99) attaches the full verdict dict at `result['verdict']` (line 98) but does NOT expose a top-level `coverage` key. The plan's claimed read path in `_review_code()` — `result.get('coverage',{}).get('retryable')` — would therefore return `None` today without the shim fix. While the plan correctly specifies this fix as work-to-do (AC#4), it also says `_review_code()` 'reads `result.get('coverage',{}).get('retryable')` directly (mirroring `_review_plan`)' as if this is the current data layout, which is inaccurate. This could cause an implementor to skip the shim fix and incorrectly believe the data is already accessible.
findings.location: Plan section 'Two data-flow shapes / Shape A / code-review translation'; AC line '_review_code() distinguishes 0/1/2/11 off the preserved coverage'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC for the taxonomy/retry/timeout documentation deliverable stated in the Integration ownership section, or explicitly descope it to a follow-on.
findings.finding: The plan's scope-exclusion claim about `runner.py` module-size allowlisting states 'ALREADY LANDED with the anole commit in this stack (visible at `--ref HEAD`)' and 'NOT a deliverable here'. This is confirmed correct — `runner.py` IS already present in `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` (line 24) and `.github/module-size-ceilings.txt` (line 29, ceiling 850). However, the plan also says 'This story owns the final runner.py reconciliation (the `preflight` `.outcome` attachment above) and the taxonomy/retry/timeout docs'. The taxonomy/retry/timeout docs ownership is stated in scope but has NO corresponding acceptance criterion. An executor cannot verify completion of this deliverable.
findings.location: Plan section 'Integration ownership (shared files)'; no corresponding AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the exit-11 ADR, explicitly state that the review-bot adapter path does not propagate the retryable posture (it BLOCKs all non-PASS verdicts including retryable-degraded ones) and confirm this is intentional design for the Gerrit integration.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the `review_bot`'s `adapter.py` 'votes off the verdict dict, tolerates open coverage keys' as an additive-safety justification for exit-11 in the ADR consumers list. This is confirmed correct (adapter.py reads `verdict.get('verdict')` and `verdict.get('coverage')`). However, the plan's ADR consumer entry also states the review-bot 'votes off the verdict dict, not exit codes'. Confirmed: the adapter calls `produce_code_review_verdict` directly (adapter.py:194), not the CLI. But the adapter IS fail-closed on ANY non-PASS verdict including INDETERMINATE — it maps INDETERMINATE to `BLOCK (coverage-gap)`. The plan does not address how the adapter treats a new degraded verdict with `coverage.resolution_class` set (which signals retryable). The adapter will BLOCK on it (treating it as coverage-gap), not retry. This is the documented behavior and may be intentional, but the plan's 'tolerates open coverage keys' framing implies no behavioral change — which is accurate for the exit-code dimension but silent on the BLOCK-vs-retry behavioral gap.
findings.location: Plan section 'Exit-code-11 contract / ADR consumers' section reference to review-bot

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the `LLMError` raised by `produce_completion_verdict` (gate_dispatch.py:877) on mid-run workflow failure: either attach `.outcome` to that raise site (e.g. by classifying `res.error` if it's an exception) or handle it as a distinct arm in `_completion_precheck` so retryable mid-run failures also reach exit 11.
findings.finding: `produce_completion_verdict` raises `LLMError` (not `LLMUnavailableError`) when the completion workflow fails mid-run, and that `LLMError` has no `.outcome` attached — so the plan's Shape-B `getattr(exc, 'outcome', None)` mechanism yields `None` for this path, always exiting 1 instead of 11 even when the underlying failure is retryable.
findings.location: Shape B data-flow section; gate_dispatch.py:875-880 (`produce_completion_verdict` raises `LLMError`); `_completion_precheck` broad `except Exception` arm
findings.suggested_fix: In `produce_completion_verdict`, if `res.error` is an exception instance, attach its `.outcome` before re-raising: `exc = LLMError(...); exc.outcome = getattr(res.error, 'outcome', None); raise exc`. Alternatively, in `_completion_precheck`, add a secondary check: `if isinstance(exc, LLMError) and not getattr(exc, 'outcome', None): _o = getattr(getattr(exc, '__cause__', None), 'outcome', None)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why returning a structured error dict (rather than raising a FastMCP `ToolError` with a structured payload) is the correct choice for the MCP LLM gate tools, and verify that the driving agent's error-handling path will correctly distinguish `{"error": ..., "retryable": true}` from a successful verdict dict.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel MCP tool error-propagation contract — returning a structured dict `{error, resolution_class, retryable, diagnostic}` on `LLMError` instead of propagating an exception as a FastMCP ToolError — without considering the MCP specification's standard tool-error mechanism or recording why this diverges from it.
findings.location: MCP structured failure return (`_mcp_llm.py`) section; AC: '_mcp_llm.py's review_plan/review_code/verify_completion return a structured failure dict … on a raised LLMError instead of propagating a FastMCP tool error'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the 'MCP structured failure return' section comparing the 'return error-dict' approach to 'raise ToolError(structured_payload)' and stating which agent-side behavior makes the dict approach preferable (e.g., 'FastMCP ToolError payloads are opaque strings at the MCP protocol level; a structured dict lets the agent branch on `retryable` without parsing'). If FastMCP supports structured ToolError bodies, verify that too.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the exit-11 ADR's Rollback section (and/or the Verdict-schema field section), explicitly assert the post-rollback persisted-record state: old readers encountering verdict dicts with the new `coverage.resolution_class`/`retryable`/`diagnostic` keys will silently ignore them because `coverage` is declared `additionalProperties:true`, ensuring zero data loss and no misrouting on rollback.
findings.finding: The plan asserts rollback is safe ('additive/rollback-safe', 'schema fields optional') but does not explicitly state the post-rollback schema STATE for verdict records that were already persisted with the new coverage fields (`resolution_class`, `retryable`, `diagnostic`) when old code re-reads them after a rollback deployment.
findings.location: Scope / Verdict-schema field section / AC: 'coverage.resolution_class … are optional properties … forward/rollback-safe'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the ADR Rollback section: 'Verdict records already written with the new coverage keys are harmless to old readers because `coverage` carries `additionalProperties:true`; no record cleanup is needed and no data loss occurs on partial or full rollback.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the home module for `_RESOLUTION_MESSAGES` (e.g., `rebar.llm.failure` alongside `ResolutionClass`, or `rebar._cli._llm_commands`) and add an import statement in the plan so shape-A and shape-B message rendering share a single source of truth.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that shape-A CLIs render the human-facing degrade message via 'a `_RESOLUTION_MESSAGES` lookup dict (the table below, keyed by class name)', and the message table IS provided in the plan. However, the plan never specifies which module or file this dict should be declared in. The executor must choose between `_llm_commands.py`, `failure.py`, a new shared module, or inline constants — but the plan gives no guidance. This is a processing-protocol/co-location gap: the decision rule for message rendering references a named object whose home is unspecified.
findings.location: Plan section 'Exit-code-11 contract / Message delivery' and 'Per-class actionable messages' table
findings.suggested_fix: Declare `_RESOLUTION_MESSAGES: dict[str, str]` in `src/rebar/llm/failure.py` immediately after the `ResolutionClass` enum definition (co-located with the class it maps), then import it in `_llm_commands.py` and in the shape-B `_completion_precheck` arm.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `str(exc)` in the MCP structured-failure `error` field must be passed through `_redact()` (or `sanitize_diagnostic({'message': str(exc)})['message']`) before being returned, and document this rule in the diagnostic-sanitization ADR.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `_mcp_llm.py` tools return `{"error": str(exc), "resolution_class": ..., "retryable": ..., "diagnostic": <sanitized>}` on a raised `LLMError`. The `diagnostic` field is explicitly sanitized (via `outcome.diagnostic` from `classify_llm_failure`), but the `error` field is `str(exc)` — a raw exception stringification. The existing redaction rules in `failure.py` (`_REDACTIONS`) handle API keys, bearer tokens, emails, hex, and base64 within the sanitizer, but `str(exc)` is NOT passed through those rules. A 401 `LLMUnavailableError` whose message contains the literal API key string, or a provider error message that embeds an email, would appear unsanitized in the `error` field returned to the MCP agent. The plan's sanitization ADR requirement covers `diagnostic` but has no specified rule for the `error` field.
findings.location: Plan section 'MCP structured failure return (`_mcp_llm.py`)' and 'Verdict-schema field'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `"error": str(exc)` with `"error": _redact(str(exc))` (importing `_redact` from `rebar.llm.failure`), or use `outcome.diagnostic.get('message', str(exc))` which is already sanitized by `classify_llm_failure`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the schema section that `coverage.resolution_class` stores the **string value** of the enum (e.g., `'WAIT_AND_RETRY'`, not the Python enum member), matching the `.value` write-site spec, and note that `ResolutionClass` is `str, Enum` so the value and name are identical.
findings.finding: The plan's Shape-A write-site spec says 'set `coverage["resolution_class"] = outcome.resolution_class.value`' (the `.value` string form of the enum), while the schema section describes the field as 'enum, 8 values' without clarifying string vs. enum-member. The `_RESOLUTION_MESSAGES` lookup dict is keyed 'by class name' — which matches `.value` (a string like `'WAIT_AND_RETRY'`). However, the AC for `_review_plan()` says it reads `coverage['retryable']` (the bool) directly. A downstream consumer that attempts `ResolutionClass(coverage['resolution_class'])` would reconstruct correctly from `.value`, but a consumer that does `coverage['resolution_class'] in ResolutionClass` would behave differently depending on whether it's a string or an enum member. The plan does not co-locate the decision rule (string `.value` vs. enum member) with the schema definition, creating an executor ambiguity.
findings.location: Plan section 'Two data-flow shapes / Shape A write-site' vs. 'Verdict-schema field'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the pre-sign fingerprint recheck spec the fallback rule for when `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` returns `'unknown'` (e.g., 'treat unknown as a mismatch — close unsigned with a stderr warning naming the unresolvable HEAD'), mirroring the pattern already used in `txn.py:370-374`.
findings.finding: The plan's pre-sign fingerprint recheck specifies: compare the `verified-at-sha:<sha>` from the manifest to `signing.head_sha(repo_root)`. The existing `signing.head_sha()` implementation returns the string `'unknown'` when the git ref cannot be resolved (non-git directory, detached HEAD, shallow clone with no HEAD). The plan does not specify the fallback decision rule when `head_sha()` returns `'unknown'`: should the recheck (a) treat 'unknown' as a mismatch and close unsigned, (b) treat 'unknown' as a match-bypass and sign anyway, or (c) raise a hard error? This is a missing fallback for an incomplete sub-step with security implications: option (b) would silently bypass the drift guard in CI/shallow-clone environments.
findings.location: Plan section 'Never-sign guard + pre-sign fingerprint recheck'
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the existing `txn.py` pattern: treat `head_sha() == 'unknown'` as a mismatch — close unsigned with the warning 'closed without a completion signature (HEAD SHA unresolvable at sign time)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prerequisite note in the `_review_code()` AC (or in the shim AC) that the `shim._verdict_to_review_result` coverage-copy must land first, and add a test that verifies `_review_code()` reads `result['coverage']['retryable']` (top-level, not `result['verdict']['coverage']['retryable']`) after the shim update.
findings.finding: The plan directs `_review_code()` to 'read `result.get("coverage",{}).get("retryable")` directly' to determine exit 11. However, `_review_code()` calls `rebar.llm.review_code()` which returns a `review_result` dict produced by `shim._verdict_to_review_result`. Today that function attaches the raw gate verdict at `result['verdict']` (a nested dict) but does NOT copy `coverage` to the top-level `result`. The plan also says the shim update 'copies the gate verdict's `coverage` (with `resolution_class`/`retryable`/`diagnostic`) to the TOP-LEVEL `review_result['coverage']`'. These two instructions are mutually dependent: the exit-11 mapping in `_review_code()` ONLY works AFTER the shim is updated. The plan states this dependency nowhere — neither in the AC ordering, nor in the scope section's call-outs. An executor implementing `_review_code()` exit-11 without first updating the shim will read `result['coverage']` as absent (the `review_result` schema has no top-level `coverage` today), silently falling back to exit 0.
findings.location: Plan section 'Two data-flow shapes / Shape A / code-review translation' and AC for `_review_code()`

findings.finding: The shape-A stderr message rendering mechanism is not described in any implementation step. The AC requires that a degraded plan-review or code-review verdict prints the class-specific message (from the table) to stderr, but the plan only states the shape-B mechanism (via `CommandError.message`) and vaguely says 'shape A as a CLI-rendered line off `coverage`' without identifying which function, at which call site, does the `resolution_class → message` lookup and `stderr` write in `_llm_commands.py`.
findings.location: What > Exit-code-11 contract > Message delivery; AC: 'A degraded verdict/close prints the class-specific message (from the table) to stderr'

findings.finding: The plan specifies shape-A stderr message delivery as 'a CLI-rendered line off coverage' without defining the rendering format — field name, separator, or human-readable template — leaving implementers to invent inconsistent output.
findings.location: Exit-code-11 contract / Message delivery section; AC: 'A degraded verdict/close prints the class-specific message (from the table) to stderr'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a shared helper (e.g. `_format_degrade_message(coverage: dict) -> str`) in `_llm_commands.py` that reads `coverage.get('resolution_class')` and returns the matching row from the table, then call it from both `_review_plan` and `_review_code`. Specify the fallback for absent/unknown class (e.g. emit the NEEDS_INVESTIGATION message).

findings.finding: The plan's 'Reachability (verified)' note asserts that the `preflight`-path `LLMUnavailableError` has `.outcome` present (parenthetical: '`.outcome` present → disposition set'). This is false. `PydanticAIRunner.preflight()` calls `_import_pydantic_ai()` and `_pai_check_config()`, which raise `LLMConfigError`/`LLMUnavailableError` directly — these exceptions are raised BEFORE the `run()` method's `except Exception` block that executes `err.outcome = classify_llm_failure(...)`. So a preflight exception will NOT carry `.outcome`, and `getattr(error, 'outcome', None)` returns `None` → NO disposition set on the preflight path.
findings.location: Shape A — plan-review + code-review return a DEGRADED VERDICT DICT / Reachability (verified) note
findings.suggested_fix: In `PydanticAIRunner.preflight()`, after catching `LLMConfigError` or before re-raising, attach: `exc.outcome = classify_llm_failure(exc)`. Or, correct the plan text to: 'the preflight path raises without `.outcome` → plain INDETERMINATE (no disposition); only the mid-run path carries `.outcome` → disposition set.'

findings.finding: The _verify_completion() CLI handler at _llm_commands.py:305 has its own `except llm.LLMError as exc: return 1` that catches LLMUnavailableError before any CommandError(returncode=11) from a deeper call can propagate. The plan's exit-11 text only describes the _completion_precheck → CommandError mechanism (the rebar close path), but the AC lists _verify_completion as an exit-11 site. The direct LLMError catch in _verify_completion is not named as an edit site in the plan body, leaving the _verify_completion exit-11 path underspecified.
findings.location: Exit-code-11 contract section: 'Shape B surfaces through TWO CLI entrypoints: rebar verify-completion (_verify_completion()) AND the rebar close / rebar transition … closed path'; AC: '_completion_precheck raises CommandError(returncode=11)'

findings.finding: The fingerprint-mismatch path ('close unsigned') has no specified exit code, but the close has already committed at that point. An agent or CI script calling `rebar close` needs to distinguish 'closed+signed' (exit 0) from 'closed+unsigned-due-to-drift' — yet the plan specifies neither. The AC only asserts 'a mismatch closes unsigned' with no returncode.
findings.location: What / Never-sign guard + pre-sign fingerprint recheck; AC: 'The pre-sign head-SHA fingerprint is re-checked inside the write lock immediately before signing; a mismatch closes unsigned (test for the mismatch path)'
findings.suggested_fix: Exit 0 with a stderr warning line is the correct choice: the ticket is legitimately closed (the write succeeded); the user must re-run `rebar review-plan` or `rebar sign` to get the attestation. Add to the AC: '...mismatch closes unsigned with exit 0 and a stderr line: Warning: closed unsigned: HEAD drifted during verify→sign window — re-sign to attest.'

findings.finding: The pre-sign fingerprint-mismatch path ('close unsigned') has no stated exit code. An executor implementing the mismatch branch in `close_ticket` must choose between exit 0 (close succeeded, just unsigned) and exit 1 (block the close), with no plan guidance. This is a defaultable gap that silently affects downstream CI and agent retry logic.
findings.location: What > Never-sign guard + pre-sign fingerprint recheck; AC: 'a mismatch closes unsigned (test for the mismatch path)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'On head-SHA mismatch, `close_ticket` closes the ticket unsigned and exits 0 (the close itself succeeded; the caller is responsible for re-verifying if desired).' Then update the AC to assert the exit code, e.g. 'a mismatch closes unsigned with exit 0; the regression test asserts returncode == 0 and asserts no `sign_manifest` call was made.'

findings.finding: runner.py is already present in both .github/module-size-allowlist.txt (line 24) and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt (line 29, ceiling=850) — the exact values the plan prescribes. The plan's deliverable 'runner.py is added to .github/module-size-allowlist.txt + module-size-ceilings.txt (850) + a docs/architecture.md remedy row' describes work that is already complete for the allowlist/ceilings portion, meaning the AC item and its verification test will silently no-op or, worse, the implementor may add a duplicate entry.
findings.location: Integration ownership section / AC: 'runner.py is added to .github/module-size-allowlist.txt + module-size-ceilings.txt (850)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC line with: 'runner.py already appears in .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and module-size-ceilings.txt (850); the remaining deliverable for this item is the docs/architecture.md remedy row (verified by test_module_size_contract.py).'

findings.finding: The plan claims runner.py needs to be 'added to `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` + `module-size-ceilings.txt` (850)' as a deliverable of this story. Both files already contain this entry: `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` is on line 24 of `module-size-allowlist.txt` and has ceiling 850 in `module-size-ceilings.txt`. The plan's 'Integration ownership' section and the corresponding AC are based on a false premise that this work remains to be done.
findings.location: Integration ownership section; AC: 'runner.py is added to `.github/module-size-allowlist.txt` + `module-size-ceilings.txt` (850)'
findings.suggested_fix: Revise the AC to: 'A `docs/architecture.md` remedy row exists for `src/rebar/llm/runner.py` (the allowlist and ceiling-850 entries are already present; verified by `test_module_size_contract.py`).'

findings.finding: The plan asserts `_review_code()` in `_llm_commands.py` is a shape-A gate that 'returns `code_review_verdict`' and can read `verdict['coverage'].get('resolution_class')` directly. In reality, `_review_code()` calls `llm.review_code()` → `shim.review_code()`, which translates the raw `code_review_verdict` into a `review_result` dict. The raw verdict is nested at `result['verdict']`, so the top-level `result['coverage']` does NOT contain `resolution_class` — the correct path is `result['verdict']['coverage'].get('resolution_class')`. The plan's stated data-access path for the exit-11 mapping at this CLI is wrong.
findings.location: Plan section 'CLI sites' and AC: 'rebar review-code (`_review_code()`, the code-review gate) applies the same shape-A exit-11 mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: In the CLI sites section, note that `_review_code()` obtains `coverage` from `result.get('verdict', {}).get('coverage') or {}` (the nested gate verdict), not `result.get('coverage') or {}`. Alternatively, specify that the exit-11 mapping should be added to the shim layer (`shim.review_code`) which already holds the raw verdict before translation.

findings.finding: `_review_code()` in `_llm_commands.py` returns a translated `review_result` dict (from `shim._verdict_to_review_result`), NOT the raw `code_review_verdict`. The plan directs the shape-A exit-11 mapping to read `verdict['coverage'].get('resolution_class')` at this CLI site, but `coverage` does not exist at the top level of the returned `review_result` — the raw gate verdict is nested as `result['verdict']`. Reading `result['coverage']` will silently return `None`, causing exit-11 to never trigger even on a retryable outage.
findings.location: What / Two data-flow shapes / Shape A; CLI arm `_review_code()` in `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` (lines 136-203); `src/rebar/llm/code_review/shim.py:_verdict_to_review_result`
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'CLI sites' section, update the shape-A path description for `_review_code()`: the resolution_class must be read as `result.get('verdict', {}).get('coverage', {}).get('resolution_class')` since `shim._verdict_to_review_result` wraps the raw gate verdict under a `'verdict'` key in the returned `review_result`. Alternatively, consider having `_review_code()` call `produce_code_review_verdict` directly (like the Gerrit adapter does) so it has the raw `code_review_verdict` — but note this changes the return shape of `rebar review-code`.

findings.finding: runner.py is already present in both .github/module-size-allowlist.txt and .github/module-size-ceilings.txt (ceiling 850). The plan says to 'add' runner.py to both files and a docs/architecture.md remedy row, treating these as new edits — but the allowlist membership and ceiling already exist.
findings.location: Scope / Integration ownership section: 'runner.py was grown by five children to 824 LOC; this story owns its module-size reconciliation — added to .github/module-size-allowlist.txt + module-size-ceilings.txt (850)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'added to .github/module-size-allowlist.txt + module-size-ceilings.txt (850)' with 'already in both files at ceiling 850; confirm the wave's edits stay within 850 or raise the ceiling and add/update the docs/architecture.md remedy row if needed'.

findings.finding: The Shape-A `_review_code()` exit-11 path must read coverage from `result["verdict"]["coverage"]`, not `result["coverage"]`, but the plan's wording 'the CLI reads the persisted `retryable` bool directly' is ambiguous about this nesting. `_review_code()` receives a `review_result` (from `shim._verdict_to_review_result`), where the raw gate verdict is nested at `result["verdict"]`; coverage fields preserved onto the `review_result` by the shim live one level deeper than a flat read would expect.
findings.location: What / Shape A — plan-review + code-review; `shim._verdict_to_review_result`; `_llm_commands.py::_review_code`
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: 'The shim preserves coverage onto the nested verdict dict already attached as `result["verdict"]`; `_review_code()` reads `result.get("verdict", {}).get("coverage", {}).get("retryable")` to determine exit 11.' No new hoisting to top-level `result["coverage"]` is needed.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
